items.q: Does a 'theme' in A-level art simply mean a subject to copy?
items.correct: No — it is an idea you investigate and interpret personally through development.
items.misconception: Yes — you find pictures of the theme and copy them accurately.
items.random_wrong: Yes — a theme is the colour scheme of the work.
items.why: Students often treat a theme as a set of images to reproduce rather than an idea to explore and develop.

items.q: Is the final outcome the only thing that matters in a thematic unit?
items.correct: No — development, experimentation and refinement are valued alongside the outcome.
items.misconception: Yes — only the final piece is marked.
items.random_wrong: No — only the written annotation is marked.
items.why: Students underinvest in development work because they assume the final piece carries all the credit.

items.correct: Testing and improving aspects such as composition, media and scale through purposeful changes.
items.misconception: Making the drawing neater and tidier.
items.why: Students equate refinement with neatness, when it really means purposeful development and improvement of the idea.

items.q: Above all, a thematic outcome should demonstrate:
items.correct: A resolved personal response developed from the chosen theme.
items.misconception: As many unrelated media as possible.
items.random_wrong: Work produced without any preparatory development.
items.why: Correct answer: A resolved personal response developed from the chosen theme.. “As many unrelated media as possible.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: It is brought to a considered, finished state that fulfils your intentions.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: It is brought to a considered, finished state that fulfils your intentions.. “It is left deliberately unfinished.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Reviewed and improved versions of an idea leading to the final piece.
items.misconception: Copying the final image directly from a single photograph.
items.random_wrong: Starting a brand-new theme each week.
items.why: It's Reviewed and improved versions of an idea leading to the final piece.. “Copying the final image directly from a single photograph.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: To judge how far it fulfils your intentions and justify your decisions.
items.random_wrong: Only to increase the word count.
items.why: It's To judge how far it fulfils your intentions and justify your decisions.. “To replace the artwork itself.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: An artist completes 3 development studies each week for 4 weeks before the final outcome. How many studies is that?
items.why: The real figure is 12 studies. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
